steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researchers say they can't attribute the 40–90% production drop specifically to honeybees because the shielding may have blocked other insect pollinators too. The correct choice must supply evidence that other insects pollinate cucumbers and were also blocked.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Other insects like solitary bees also pollinate onion plants, and some studies have allowed them to pollinate normally while excluding honeybees.’: Onions are a different crop and irrelevant to explaining the cucumber experiment's confound. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Other insects like squash bees also pollinate cucumber plants, and the shielding in the first phase of the study blocked them as well.’: This directly establishes the confound — another pollinator was excluded alongside honeybees, so the experiment cannot isolate honeybee-specific effects. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘Honeybees are more commonly used to pollinate cucumber plants than onion plants.’: How often honeybees are used generally does not explain why the shielded experiment conflated multiple pollinator types. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘The shielding used in the first phase of the study did not affect the amount of sunlight the cucumber plants received.’: Sunlight is unrelated to whether the shielding blocked non-honeybee pollinators. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
